# Project Charta
## Context and Scope

This project focuses on the socio-economic and geographic analysis of meteorite discoveries worldwide. While NASA records where meteorites were found, the data is heavily biased by human presence and environmental conditions.
The "Meteorite Treasure Map" aims to explain the "Discovery Gap": Why do we find thousands of meteorites in the Sahara or Antarctica, but almost none in the Black Forest? By correlating NASA landing data with population density, GDP, and land cover, we visualize the Discovery Probability. The goal is to shift the perspective from where meteorites landed to why we found them there, ultimately creating an "Opportunity Map" for future discoveries.

## Project Objectives and Success Criteria
Objective:
Enable users to understand that meteorite distribution maps are often maps of human activity and geography. The project identifies "Discovery Gaps" by overlaying NASA data with socio-economic and environmental layers.

Success Criteria:
- Users can identify the correlation between population density and discovery rates.
- Users can distinguish between "High-Potential" (arid, flat) and "Low-Visibility" (forest, urban) zones.
- The visualization successfully highlights regions where meteorites are likely hidden but unrecorded

Quantitative criteria:
- At least 4 clear visualizations covering location, mass, class, and time.
- Data includes more than 100 observations and at least 6 features.

Out of Scope:
- No real-time data updates.
- No advanced predictive modeling or machine learning.

## Stakeholder Analysis
Stakeholders include:

- Project team members: responsible for data analysis and visualization.
- Lecturers: evaluate the project and provide feedback.
- Students and general public: end users of the visualization.

The project team develops the product, lecturers assess its quality, and users benefit from understanding the data.

## User Analysis

PERSONA 1
Name: Lukas (Citizen Scientist)
Age: 29

![ ](pics/lukas.jpeg){ width=90% }

Background & Behavior
Software developer with a strong interest in astronomy.
Lives in a forested region in Southern Germany with low meteorite recovery rates.
Approaches problems through data analysis and pattern recognition.

Goals

- Validate detection bias caused by vegetation and soil coverage
- Demonstrate that absence of finds does not imply absence of meteorites
- Understand and communicate population-driven data bias

Pain Points

- Binary map logic (find vs. no find) lacks probabilistic context
- No information about discovery context (accidental vs. systematic findings)
- Misleading spatial patterns due to uneven human activity

Key Features

- Detection Probability Map
- Visualizes likelihood of meteorite discovery based on surface conditions
- Population Bias Adjustment
- Normalizes find density relative to human presence

Local Impact Estimator
- Estimates expected meteorite falls vs. actual detection probability

PERSONA 2
Name:Dr. Aris (Expedition Planner)
Age: 45

![ ](pics/aris.jpeg){ width=200px }

Background & Behavior
Field geologist planning high-cost meteorite expeditions.
Decision-making is driven by risk reduction and expected return.

Goals
- Maximize return on field investigation
- Identify under-explored but high-potential regions
- Optimize timing and logistics based on environmental conditions

Pain Points

- Fragmented data across multiple platforms
- High financial risk due to poor site selection
- No integrated way to evaluate terrain suitability and search history

Key Features

- Opportunity Map (Bivariate Analysis)
- Combines low population density with high aridity

Geological Stability Filter
- Highlights surfaces with long-term preservation potential

Accessibility Index
- Estimates logistical effort based on distance to infrastructure





## Situation Assessment
Resources:
- Dataset containing meteorite landings with attributes such as mass, location, class, and year.
- Scientific Data: NASA Meteorite Landings.
- Socio-Economic Data: World Bank (GDP per capita, Population Density).
- Environmental Data: MODIS Land Cover and Aridity Index (to calculate visibility and preservation).
- Team members with basic Python and data visualization knowledge.
- Tools: Python, Quarto, GitHub.

Constraints:
- Limited time until project deadlines.
- Requirement for reproducibility.

Risks:
- Data quality issues (missing values, inconsistencies)
- GDP/Population data might be at a country level while meteorites are point-based (requires spatial joining).
- Technical challenges in visualization or deployment

## Visualization Concept
The visualization product is designed to translate the project objectives into an intuitive and accessible representation of meteorite data. It enables users to explore patterns in meteorite distribution, mass, and classification in a structured and visually clear way, addressing their need for simple and understandable insights.

Product form:
The chosen format is a data story presented as a web-based application using Quarto. This format allows a guided narrative, where users are introduced step by step to the most important aspects of the dataset. At the same time, it allows light exploration, combining storytelling with elements of an exploratory tool. It follows a narrative: from the "Illusion" (NASA's raw map) to the "Reality" (Adjusted for human and geographic bias).

Visual encodings:

- Bivariate Choropleth Map: To show the intersection of population density and discovery frequency.
- Correlation Plots: GDP vs. Number of recorded finds to check for "Wealth Bias."
- Heatmap of the "Discovery Gap": Highlighting arid, uninhabited areas with high preservation potential.
- Scatterplot: Mass vs. Discovery Year, filtered by Land Cover type.

These visual encodings are chosen to represent key analytical dimensions such as distribution, comparison, location, and temporal trends.

Interactivity:

Layer Toggles: Switch between "Raw Finds" and "Discovery Probability."

Region Inspector: Click a region to see its "Discovery Efficiency Score" (based on Aridity and Population).

Narrative and annotation:
The visualization follows a clear storytelling structure: introduction, exploration of key variables, and summary of findings. Each visualization is accompanied by short explanatory text and annotations to guide the user and highlight key insights. This reduces cognitive load and helps users interpret the data correctly.

Target medium and integration:
The visualization product will be published as a web-based application using GitHub Pages. This ensures easy accessibility across devices such as laptops and tablets without requiring additional software installation.

Value of the design:

Cognitive and analytical value:
The selected visual encodings enhance pattern recognition and analytical understanding. The map supports spatial reasoning, the histogram reveals distributions and outliers, the bar chart enables comparison, and the line chart highlights trends over time. Interactivity further supports exploration and deeper insight.

Communicative value:
The use of simple, well-known chart types combined with a structured narrative ensures that the information is communicated clearly to a broad audience. Annotations and explanations help users with limited data literacy understand the key insights.

Experiential and aesthetic value:
A clean and minimal visual design combined with light interactivity creates an engaging user experience. The ability to explore the data (e.g. through tooltips or zooming) increases user involvement and trust in the visualization, while maintaining clarity and usability.

Justification:
The visualization concept directly supports the project objectives by enabling users to understand meteorite patterns in an intuitive and structured way. It addresses user needs by simplifying complex data, reducing cognitive effort, and providing both guided storytelling and limited interactive exploration.



## Project Plan
Divide the project into individual phases, describe them briefly and draw up a preliminary timetable, e.g. as a Gantt chart:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-project-plan
%%| fig-cap: Preliminary project plan in the form of a Gantt chart.
gantt
    title Project Plan
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    
    section Project Understanding
        Project setup            :a1, 2026-03-18, 2d
        Project charta           :a2, 2026-03-19, 1d

    section Data Exploration
        Data cleaning            :a3, 2026-03-21, 5d
        Data analysis            :a4, 2026-03-26, 5d

    section Visualization
        Create visualizations    :a5, 2026-04-01, 10d
        Build data story         :a6, 2026-04-11, 7d

    section Finalization
        Documentation            :a7, 2026-05-01, 7d
        Presentation prep        :a8, 2026-05-20, 5d
```

See [Mermaid syntax for Gantt charts](https://mermaid.js.org/syntax/gantt.html). It might not be displayed correctly in Safari &#8594; use Chrome. [Live editor with export functionality](https://mermaid.live/)

## Roles and Contact Details
List the people involved in the development work here with their role titles, tasks and contact details.
Team Members

- Baskaran Amutha (baskaamu): Technical Setup and Data Integration
- Eros Palumbo (palero01): Project Lead and Concept & Socio-Economic Analysis.
- Nicolas Schwaiger (schwani8): Design, Visualization & Layout Support.
